title: Otitis Externa
oneLiner: Acute infection/inflammation of the external auditory canal; common in swimmers, diabetics, and eczema sufferers; can cause severe pain and rarely progress to life-threatening skull base osteomyelitis.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**
A 28-year-old woman presents to the ED with a 3-day history of severe right ear pain that worsens when she lies on that side. She went swimming two days ago and has been using cotton buds to dry her ear. On examination, she winces the moment you touch the tragus. The canal is erythematous, oedematous, and filled with moist debris. The tympanic membrane is normal but difficult to visualise due to canal swelling. Her hearing is slightly muffled.

**Vignette Dissection:**

- *Severe ear pain + tragal tenderness* → hallmark of external ear canal inflammation; differentiates from otitis media (which has no tragal pain).
- *Worse when lying on that side* → pressure from pillow on the inflamed pinna/canal.
- *Swimming + cotton buds* → classic moisture + microtrauma triggers.
- *Canal oedema, erythema, debris* → infected cerumen, desquamated epithelium, and pus.
- *TM not visible* → canal swelling can obscure view; requires careful cleaning.
- *Muffled hearing* → conductive loss from canal obstruction, not TM or middle ear.

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

1. **The diabetic with "just a sore ear"** – mild pain, minimal discharge, but weeks of symptoms. Canal may look fairly clean. This is a red flag for *malignant otitis externa* (skull base osteomyelitis) – needs urgent CT and ENT referral. Don’t dismiss it as ordinary infection.
2. **The eczematous patient with bilateral itching** – chronic, itchy, scaly canals, minimal pain, no purulence. This is *eczematous otitis externa* (or fungal superinfection). Topical steroids ± antifungals, not antibiotics.
3. **The child with ear pulling and fever** – could be otitis media or externa. Clue: if you can pull the pinna without wincing, it’s more likely media. But children often have both (otorrhea from perforated OM can macerate canal and cause secondary OE).
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-Language Definition:**
Otitis externa (OE) is an inflammatory condition of the external auditory canal skin (and sometimes the pinna). Usually infectious (bacterial > fungal), but can be allergic or eczematous. Essentially “swimmer’s ear” gone wrong.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Extremely common – up to 10% of people will have at least one episode. Peak in summer, swimmers, hot humid climates.
- **Stakes:** Usually trivial, but can destroy quality of life from pain. In immunocompromised patients (diabetes, HIV, elderly), it can progress to malignant OE – a skull base osteomyelitis with cranial nerve palsies and death if missed.
- **Common Pitfalls:** 
  - Mistaking OE for otitis media → wrong treatment (oral antibiotics don’t penetrate canal)
  - Using systemic antibiotics when topical suffices
  - Not cleaning the canal → drops can’t reach infected skin
  - Missing early malignant OE because “pain isn’t that bad yet”
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story:**

The external auditory canal is lined by thin skin that continuously migrates cerumen and desquamated epithelium outward (like a slow conveyor belt). This keeps the canal clean and dry. Break this barrier (moisture, trauma, eczema) and bacteria (normally *Pseudomonas*, *Staph aureus*) overgrow. Infection → inflammation → oedema → occlusion → pain → more moisture → vicious cycle.

**Mechanism → Sign:**

- **Infection triggers vasodilation and plasma exudation** → canal skin becomes erythematous (red) and oedematous (swollen). Swelling narrows the lumen → conductive hearing loss.
- **Prostaglandins and bradykinin** → intense pain. Since canal skin is tightly adherent to perichondrium and bone (no loose subcutaneous tissue), even small amounts of oedema cause severe stretching. Hence *tragal tenderness* – moving the cartilaginous canal pulls on inflamed skin.
- **Bacterial toxins + desquamated cells → purulent debris.** Moisture from swimming/sweating macerates skin, making it easier for bacteria to penetrate.
- **Fungal overgrowth (e.g. *Aspergillus*, *Candida*)** → itching and minimal pain because fungi don’t provoke the same acute inflammatory cytokine storm. Colony visible as black/white/grey sporulation (like old bread mould).
- **Chronic eczema** → itching, scaling, lichenification → skin barrier disrupted → secondary bacterial or fungal infection.

**Connect to Pattern Recognition:**
- The patient who swims → moisture + microtrauma → *Pseudomonas* (most common cause in swimmers).
- The diabetic with minimal canal findings but persistent pain → *Pseudomonas* extends through the bony canal floor (Santorini’s fissures) into skull base → osteomyelitis. Pain out of proportion to inflammation is the clue.
- The eczematous patient with bilateral itching → no acute infection; primary dermatosis with secondary eczematous changes.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Cause | Typical Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-------------------|
| **Infectious – Bacterial** | *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* (most common) | “Swimmer / hot tub / humid environment” |
|  | *Staphylococcus aureus* | “Folliculitis / recent ear piercing / contact with infected person” |
|  | Anaerobes (rare, in necrotising OE) | “Foul-smelling discharge + diabetic/immunocompromised” |
| **Infectious – Fungal** | *Aspergillus niger* | “Black specks in debris / chronic itching / recent antibiotics” |
|  | *Candida albicans* | “White plaque / immunocompromised / diabetic” |
| **Allergic / Irritant** | Contact dermatitis (nickel, ear drops, hair dye) | “After using new earrings / hair product / ear drops” |
|  | Topical antibiotic allergy (neomycin) | “Worsening itching and erythema after 3–4 days of treatment” |
| **Eczematous** | Atopic or seborrheic dermatitis | “Bilateral itching, scaling in ears and scalp” |
| **Traumatic** | Cotton buds, fingernails, hearing aids | “Cleans ears with cotton bud / uses hearing aid daily” |
| **Systemic / Metabolic** | Diabetes mellitus | “Recurrent / bilateral / severe or malignant OE” |
|  | Immunosuppression (HIV, chemo) | “Opportunistic infection / fungal overgrowth” |

**Most Common Cause:** Acute bacterial otitis externa from *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* in a setting of moisture (swimming, sweating, high humidity).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria (Clinical Diagnosis – No Lab Needed in Typical Cases):**
- **Key triad:**
  1. Pain on manipulation of pinna/tragus (tragal tenderness)
  2. Erythema and oedema of canal skin
  3. Debris or discharge in canal
- *Plus* normal TM (if visible) and no mastoid tenderness.

**When to investigate further:**

| Test / Procedure | What It Shows | Why Order? | Interpretation |
|-----------------|---------------|------------|----------------|
| **Ear swab for micro + culture** | Causative organism and sensitivities | Only if: recalcitrant, immunocompromised, suspected fungal, or recurrent | *Pseudomonas* → treat with ciprofloxacin drops; *Staph* → neomycin/fusidic; *Aspergillus* → clotrimazole. Resistant strains take weeks |
| **CT temporal bone** | Bone erosion, soft tissue extension | Suspected **malignant OE** – skull base osteomyelitis | Patchy erosion of mastoid/tympanic floor = bad. Normal CT doesn’t rule out early disease |
| **MRI brain + internal auditory meatus** | Cranial nerve enhancement (VII, IX, X, XI, XII) | Cranial nerve deficits (facial palsy, hoarseness, shoulder drop) | Confirms spread to skull base foramina. Urgent ENT and ID consult |
| **Audiometry** | Conductive vs sensorineural hearing loss | Baseline for documentation, especially if severe or recurrent | Conductive loss = debris/oedema. Sensorineural loss suggests labyrinthine involvement (rare) |

**Pattern-Recognition: What Single Finding Confirms / Rules Out?**

- **Confirmatory sign (rule-in):** Tragal tenderness on gentle pressure = almost 100% specific for OE (except in pinna perichondritis – rare). If you can pull the pinna without pain, it’s NOT OE.
- **Rule-out test:** If the patient has no tragal tenderness, think otitis media, mastoiditis, or dental pain. Also check TM (if visible) – normal TM makes primary OE, but do not rely on TM if canal is too swollen.
- **Red-flag laboratory finding:** Elevated ESR/CRP in OE is not typical for simple infection. If CRP > 50 and pain persists, think malignancy.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (First Hour in ED or Clinic)**

- **Aural Toilet** (gentle suction or dry mopping) – **most important step**. Debris must be removed so topical drops can reach infected skin. Without cleaning, drops can’t work.
- **Topical antibiotic drops** – first-line:
  - *Ciprofloxacin 0.3% otic drops*: 3 drops into affected ear twice daily for 7 days. Why: covers *Pseudomonas* and *Staph* well, and has no ototoxicity (unlike aminoglycosides).
  - Alternative: *Gentamicin or Neomycin drops*: only if TM intact – aminoglycosides can damage cochlea if middle ear exposed. Many guidelines prefer quinolones to avoid this risk.
- **Analgesia** – paracetamol 1g PO qds + ibuprofen 400mg PO tds (if no contraindication). Pain is severe, so don’t skimp.
- **Ear wick** (e.g. ribbon gauze or Pope wick) if canal is too swollen to accept drops: insert wick, then saturate with drops. Wick keeps canal open and delivers drug.

**Ward / Outpatient (24–72 hours)**

- **Reassess in 2–3 days** – subjective improvement, reduced pain, better canal visualisation. If not improving, re-culture, consider fungal cause, or refer ENT.
- **Continue drops** for full 7 days (do not stop early even if better – risk of relapse or fungal overgrowth).
- **Avoid water in ear** – use cotton ball with Vaseline when showering; abstain from swimming for at least 2 weeks.
- **Advice on no cotton buds** – trauma perpetuates infection.
- **If fungal confirmed** (or empirical suspicion in chronic cases): *Clotrimazole 1% drops* 3 drops twice daily for 10 days. Do not use antibiotics.
- **If eczematous component** – add topical steroid: *Hydrocortisone 1% otic drops* (or combine with antibiotic in Locorten-Vioform). Steroids reduce inflammation and itching, break the itch-scratch cycle.

**Long-Term**

- **Prevent recurrence**: dry ears after swimming with a hairdryer on low/cool setting; avoid inserting anything; use acetic acid 2% drops after swimming (acidifies canal, inhibits *Pseudomonas*).
- **Manage underlying dermatosis** – if atopic dermatitis, use emollients and topical steroids as per dermatology.
- **For diabetics**: tight blood sugar control reduces risk of malignant OE.
- **Hearing aid users**: clean device and ear moulds regularly; consider frequent breaks from hearing aid to allow canal to air out.

**What NOT to do (Common Pitfalls)**

- Do NOT give oral antibiotics for simple OE – they do not penetrate canal skin. Only use oral if systemic signs (fever, cellulitis beyond ear) or immunocompromised.
- Do NOT use aminoglycoside drops if TM could be perforated – risk of ototoxicity (cochlear damage). If TM not visible, assume perforation until otherwise, so use ciprofloxacin.
- Do NOT use ear candles – they deposit wax deeper and can burn skin.
- Do NOT forget to clean the canal before drops. “Drops on debris” = placebo.
- Do NOT keep treating with antibiotics if no improvement after 3 days – think resistant organism, fungal, or malignant OE.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Otitis Externa | Otitis Media (Acute) | Mastoiditis | Perichondritis of Pinna |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|-------------|-------------------------|
| **Pain** | Severe, **worse when pulling pinna** (tragal tenderness) | Deep ear pain, **no tragal tenderness**; worse when lying flat | Post-auricular pain, **tenderness over mastoid** | Pinna pain, **worse when touching pinna cartilage**, not tragus |
| **Exam** | Canal erythema/oedema, debris; TM normal if visible | TM bulging, red, with or without perforation; canal normal | Swelling behind ear, loss of post-auricular sulcus, TM may be bulging | Erythema and swelling of pinna, sparing lobule; canal may be normal |
| **Hearing loss** | Conductive (canal obstruction) | Conductive (TM effusion) | Conductive (middle ear) | None unless canal involved |
| **ECG / vital signs** | Normal; afebrile | Often febrile (children) | High fever, toxic appearance | Low-grade fever if infected |
| **Labs (if obtained)** | Normal WBC (unless severe) | Elevated WBC, CRP | Elevated WBC, CRP | Usually normal |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | Tragal tenderness + normal TM | No tragal tenderness + abnormal TM | Post-auricular swelling + TM abnormality | Pinna cartilage involvement, lobule spared (hallmark of cellulitis vs perichondritis) |

**Key Differentiator:** Tragal tenderness is pathognomonic for OE. If absent, look elsewhere.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major Complications:**

1. **Malignant (Necrotising) Otitis Externa** – infection spreads from canal to skull base (temporal bone osteomyelitis).  
   - **Who gets it:** Diabetics, elderly, immunocompromised (HIV, chemo).  
   - **How it presents:** Persistent ear pain >2 weeks unresponsive to treatment; granulation tissue in canal (especially at the floor); later, cranial nerve palsies – facial (VII) most common, then glossopharyngeal (IX), vagus (X), accessory (XI), hypoglossal (XII).  
   - **Key signs:** Granulation tissue in deep canal, facial nerve palsy, headache, fever.  
   - **What to do:** Urgent admit, CT temporal bone, MRI if CN deficits, ENT consult, IV antibiotics (anti-pseudomonal, e.g. ceftazidime or piperacillin-tazobactam), often need surgical debridement.  
   - **Late presentation (“what happens if untreated”):** Cranial nerve palsy that does not recover; extension to contralateral skull base; meningitis, brain abscess, death.

2. **Chronic Otitis Externa** – persistent inflammation from undertreated infection, fungal overgrowth, or skin disease.  
   - **Presentation:** Itching, mild pain, scaling, thick debris for weeks to months.  
   - **Key signs:** Canal skin is lichenified, stenotic; no granulation.  
   - **What to do:** Culture, treat fungus if present, topical steroid + antifungal, avoid moisture.

3. **Myringitis / Perforation of TM** – rarely, severe OE can cause TM inflammation (myringitis bullosa) with bullae that rupture.  
   - **Presentation:** Sudden sharp pain followed by bloody otorrhea.  
   - **Key signs:** TM bullae (if you can see), or perforation.  
   - **What to do:** Usually self-limiting; keep ear dry, avoid drops containing ototoxic drugs (use ciprofloxacin only).

4. **Furuncle of the Canal** – localised abscess in outer cartilaginous canal (hair follicle).  
   - **Presentation:** Severe pain, can be mistaken for OE.  
   - **Key signs:** A single, localized, painful swelling in the lateral canal; no diffuse oedema.  
   - **What to do:** Incision and drainage, topical or oral anti-staphylococcal antibiotic (e.g. flucloxacillin 500mg PO qds for 5 days).

**Late-Presentation Pattern (Exam Stem):**  
A 68-year-old diabetic man with 3 weeks of right ear pain, treated by GP with neomycin drops twice, still painful. Now has mild right facial droop. What is the diagnosis and next step? → Malignant OE. Urgent CT temporal bone + ENT referral.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **TRAGUS:** Tragal tenderness, Red canal, Abscess (localised), Granulation (warning), Underlying skin disease, Swimmer’s history. → Think OE.
- **MEDS for Otitis Externa:**  
  - **M**oisture avoid  
  - **E**ar wick if occluded  
  - **D**rops (ciprofloxacin)  
  - **S**teroids for itching

**Five–Seven Clinical Pearls:**

1. The most reliable sign to differentiate OE from OM is **tragal tenderness** – not TM appearance (which you may not see).
2. If you suspect malignant OE, the earliest sign is **granulation tissue at the floor of the canal** – look for it during otoscopy.
3. Cotton buds are the enemy – they push debris deeper, macerate skin, and cause trauma. Ask patients to stop.
4. In chronic OE unresponsive to antibiotics, culture for **Aspergillus** (look for black fungal colonies). Treat with clotrimazole drops.
5. Never use antibiotic drops containing **aminoglycosides (gentamicin, neomycin, framycetin)** if there is any chance of TM perforation – risk of permanent hearing loss. Ciprofloxacin is safe.
6. **Icicles and swimming** – ear plugs + a cap reduce risk, but drying canals with a hairdryer on low is even more effective.
7. **Topical steroid alone (e.g. Locorten-Vioform)** is curative for many mild cases of OE because inflammation is the main driver; antibiotics are only needed if frank infection (purulent discharge, pus).

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**

- ❌ Prescribing oral antibiotics for OE without trying topical first.
- ❌ Forgetting to remove debris before drops – drops cannot penetrate a lump of wax.
- ❌ Assuming bilateral ear pain is OE; think systemic dermatosis (eczema, psoriasis) or fungal.
- ❌ Missing facial nerve palsy in a diabetic with ear pain – immediate referral.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to Call Senior / ENT / ICU:**

| Situation | Trigger | Action |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| **Suspected malignant OE** | Any of: diabetic/immunocompromised + severe pain >2 weeks + granulation tissue in canal + cranial nerve palsy (especially VII). | Urgent ENT referral, CT temporal bone, IV antibiotics, blood cultures, strict glucose control. ENT will likely arrange MRI and surgery. |
| **Facial nerve palsy** | New-onset ipsilateral facial weakness + ear symptoms. | Same as above – malignant OE until proven otherwise. |
| **Systemic sepsis** | Fever >38.5°C, rigors, hypotension, raised lactate, high NEWS2 (>5). | Admit, IV fluids, IV antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam), septic screen, ICU if shock. |
| **Meningism** | Headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, change in consciousness. | Meningitis may complicate malignant OE. Urgent CT head + lumbar puncture, IV antibiotics covering *Pseudomonas* (e.g. ceftazidime + metronidazole), ICU if deteriorating. |
| **Airway concern** | Massive swelling of pinna and periauricular tissue causing trismus or dysphagia. | Rare, but possible in severe cellulitis. High dependency unit, IV antibiotics, ENT for possible drainage. |

**Specific Thresholds to Monitor:**
- **NEWS2**: Score ≥5 → physician review; ≥7 → critical care outreach.
- **Lactate**: >2 mmol/L – consider sepsis; >4 – likely septic shock.
- **GCS**: ≤14 – assess for intracranial spread.
- **CRP**: Rising despite 48h of topical treatment → consider systemic infection or malignant OE.

**When in doubt, ENT is your friend – a phone call early is better than a crisis later.**

## Otitis Externa
**One-liner:** Acute infection/inflammation of the external auditory canal; common in swimmers, diabetics, and eczema sufferers; can cause severe pain and rarely progress to life-threatening skull base osteomyelitis.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**
A 28-year-old woman presents to the ED with a 3-day history of severe right ear pain that worsens when she lies on that side. She went swimming two days ago and has been using cotton buds to dry her ear. On examination, she winces the moment you touch the tragus. The canal is erythematous, oedematous, and filled with moist debris. The tympanic membrane is normal but difficult to visualise due to canal swelling. Her hearing is slightly muffled.

**Vignette Dissection:**

- *Severe ear pain + tragal tenderness* → hallmark of external ear canal inflammation; differentiates from otitis media (which has no tragal pain).
- *Worse when lying on that side* → pressure from pillow on the inflamed pinna/canal.
- *Swimming + cotton buds* → classic moisture + microtrauma triggers.
- *Canal oedema, erythema, debris* → infected cerumen, desquamated epithelium, and pus.
- *TM not visible* → canal swelling can obscure view; requires careful cleaning.
- *Muffled hearing* → conductive loss from canal obstruction, not TM or middle ear.

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

1. **The diabetic with "just a sore ear"** – mild pain, minimal discharge, but weeks of symptoms. Canal may look fairly clean. This is a red flag for *malignant otitis externa* (skull base osteomyelitis) – needs urgent CT and ENT referral. Don’t dismiss it as ordinary infection.
2. **The eczematous patient with bilateral itching** – chronic, itchy, scaly canals, minimal pain, no purulence. This is *eczematous otitis externa* (or fungal superinfection). Topical steroids ± antifungals, not antibiotics.
3. **The child with ear pulling and fever** – could be otitis media or externa. Clue: if you can pull the pinna without wincing, it’s more likely media. But children often have both (otorrhea from perforated OM can macerate canal and cause secondary OE).

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-Language Definition:**
Otitis externa (OE) is an inflammatory condition of the external auditory canal skin (and sometimes the pinna). Usually infectious (bacterial > fungal), but can be allergic or eczematous. Essentially “swimmer’s ear” gone wrong.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Extremely common – up to 10% of people will have at least one episode. Peak in summer, swimmers, hot humid climates.
- **Stakes:** Usually trivial, but can destroy quality of life from pain. In immunocompromised patients (diabetes, HIV, elderly), it can progress to malignant OE – a skull base osteomyelitis with cranial nerve palsies and death if missed.
- **Common Pitfalls:** 
  - Mistaking OE for otitis media → wrong treatment (oral antibiotics don’t penetrate canal)
  - Using systemic antibiotics when topical suffices
  - Not cleaning the canal → drops can’t reach infected skin
  - Missing early malignant OE because “pain isn’t that bad yet”

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The Story:**

The external auditory canal is lined by thin skin that continuously migrates cerumen and desquamated epithelium outward (like a slow conveyor belt). This keeps the canal clean and dry. Break this barrier (moisture, trauma, eczema) and bacteria (normally *Pseudomonas*, *Staph aureus*) overgrow. Infection → inflammation → oedema → occlusion → pain → more moisture → vicious cycle.

**Mechanism → Sign:**

- **Infection triggers vasodilation and plasma exudation** → canal skin becomes erythematous (red) and oedematous (swollen). Swelling narrows the lumen → conductive hearing loss.
- **Prostaglandins and bradykinin** → intense pain. Since canal skin is tightly adherent to perichondrium and bone (no loose subcutaneous tissue), even small amounts of oedema cause severe stretching. Hence *tragal tenderness* – moving the cartilaginous canal pulls on inflamed skin.
- **Bacterial toxins + desquamated cells → purulent debris.** Moisture from swimming/sweating macerates skin, making it easier for bacteria to penetrate.
- **Fungal overgrowth (e.g. *Aspergillus*, *Candida*)** → itching and minimal pain because fungi don’t provoke the same acute inflammatory cytokine storm. Colony visible as black/white/grey sporulation (like old bread mould).
- **Chronic eczema** → itching, scaling, lichenification → skin barrier disrupted → secondary bacterial or fungal infection.

**Connect to Pattern Recognition:**
- The patient who swims → moisture + microtrauma → *Pseudomonas* (most common cause in swimmers).
- The diabetic with minimal canal findings but persistent pain → *Pseudomonas* extends through the bony canal floor (Santorini’s fissures) into skull base → osteomyelitis. Pain out of proportion to inflammation is the clue.
- The eczematous patient with bilateral itching → no acute infection; primary dermatosis with secondary eczematous changes.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Cause | Typical Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-------------------|
| **Infectious – Bacterial** | *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* (most common) | “Swimmer / hot tub / humid environment” |
|  | *Staphylococcus aureus* | “Folliculitis / recent ear piercing / contact with infected person” |
|  | Anaerobes (rare, in necrotising OE) | “Foul-smelling discharge + diabetic/immunocompromised” |
| **Infectious – Fungal** | *Aspergillus niger* | “Black specks in debris / chronic itching / recent antibiotics” |
|  | *Candida albicans* | “White plaque / immunocompromised / diabetic” |
| **Allergic / Irritant** | Contact dermatitis (nickel, ear drops, hair dye) | “After using new earrings / hair product / ear drops” |
|  | Topical antibiotic allergy (neomycin) | “Worsening itching and erythema after 3–4 days of treatment” |
| **Eczematous** | Atopic or seborrheic dermatitis | “Bilateral itching, scaling in ears and scalp” |
| **Traumatic** | Cotton buds, fingernails, hearing aids | “Cleans ears with cotton bud / uses hearing aid daily” |
| **Systemic / Metabolic** | Diabetes mellitus | “Recurrent / bilateral / severe or malignant OE” |
|  | Immunosuppression (HIV, chemo) | “Opportunistic infection / fungal overgrowth” |

**Most Common Cause:** Acute bacterial otitis externa from *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* in a setting of moisture (swimming, sweating, high humidity).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria (Clinical Diagnosis – No Lab Needed in Typical Cases):**

**When to investigate further:**

| Test / Procedure | What It Shows | Why Order? | Interpretation |
|-----------------|---------------|------------|----------------|

- **Aural Toilet** (gentle suction or dry mopping) – **most important step**. Debris must be removed so topical drops can reach infected skin. Without cleaning, drops can’t work.

**Ward / Outpatient (24–72 hours)**

- **Reassess in 2–3 days** – subjective improvement, reduced pain, better canal visualisation. If not improving, re-culture, consider fungal cause, or refer ENT.
- **Continue drops** for full 7 days (do not stop early even if better – risk of relapse or fungal overgrowth).
- **Avoid water in ear** – use cotton ball with Vaseline when showering; abstain from swimming for at least 2 weeks.
- **Advice on no cotton buds** – trauma perpetuates infection.
- **If fungal confirmed** (or empirical suspicion in chronic cases): *Clotrimazole 1% drops* 3 drops twice daily for 10 days. Do not use antibiotics.
- **If eczematous component** – add topical steroid: *Hydrocortisone 1% otic drops* (or combine with antibiotic in Locorten-Vioform). Steroids reduce inflammation and itching, break the itch-scratch cycle.

- **Hearing aid users**: clean device and ear moulds regularly; consider frequent breaks from hearing aid to allow canal to air out.

**What NOT to do (Common Pitfalls)**

- Do NOT give oral antibiotics for simple OE – they do not penetrate canal skin. Only use oral if systemic signs (fever, cellulitis beyond ear) or immunocompromised.
- Do NOT use aminoglycoside drops if TM could be perforated – risk of ototoxicity (cochlear damage). If TM not visible, assume perforation until otherwise, so use ciprofloxacin.
- Do NOT use ear candles – they deposit wax deeper and can burn skin.
- Do NOT forget to clean the canal before drops. “Drops on debris” = placebo.
- Do NOT keep treating with antibiotics if no improvement after 3 days – think resistant organism, fungal, or malignant OE.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Otitis Externa | Otitis Media (Acute) | Mastoiditis | Perichondritis of Pinna |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|-------------|-------------------------|
| **Pain** | Severe, **worse when pulling pinna** (tragal tenderness) | Deep ear pain, **no tragal tenderness**; worse when lying flat | Post-auricular pain, **tenderness over mastoid** | Pinna pain, **worse when touching pinna cartilage**, not tragus |
| **Exam** | Canal erythema/oedema, debris; TM normal if visible | TM bulging, red, with or without perforation; canal normal | Swelling behind ear, loss of post-auricular sulcus, TM may be bulging | Erythema and swelling of pinna, sparing lobule; canal may be normal |
| **Hearing loss** | Conductive (canal obstruction) | Conductive (TM effusion) | Conductive (middle ear) | None unless canal involved |
| **ECG / vital signs** | Normal; afebrile | Often febrile (children) | High fever, toxic appearance | Low-grade fever if infected |
| **Labs (if obtained)** | Normal WBC (unless severe) | Elevated WBC, CRP | Elevated WBC, CRP | Usually normal |
| **Key distinguishing feature** | Tragal tenderness + normal TM | No tragal tenderness + abnormal TM | Post-auricular swelling + TM abnormality | Pinna cartilage involvement, lobule spared (hallmark of cellulitis vs perichondritis) |

**Key Differentiator:** Tragal tenderness is pathognomonic for OE. If absent, look elsewhere.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major Complications:**

1. **Malignant (Necrotising) Otitis Externa** – infection spreads from canal to skull base (temporal bone osteomyelitis).  
   - **Who gets it:** Diabetics, elderly, immunocompromised (HIV, chemo).  
   - **How it presents:** Persistent ear pain >2 weeks unresponsive to treatment; granulation tissue in canal (especially at the floor); later, cranial nerve palsies – facial (VII) most common, then glossopharyngeal (IX), vagus (X), accessory (XI), hypoglossal (XII).  
   - **Key signs:** Granulation tissue in deep canal, facial nerve palsy, headache, fever.  
   - **What to do:** Urgent admit, CT temporal bone, MRI if CN deficits, ENT consult, IV antibiotics (anti-pseudomonal, e.g. ceftazidime or piperacillin-tazobactam), often need surgical debridement.  
   - **Late presentation (“what happens if untreated”):** Cranial nerve palsy that does not recover; extension to contralateral skull base; meningitis, brain abscess, death.

2. **Chronic Otitis Externa** – persistent inflammation from undertreated infection, fungal overgrowth, or skin disease.  
   - **Presentation:** Itching, mild pain, scaling, thick debris for weeks to months.  
   - **Key signs:** Canal skin is lichenified, stenotic; no granulation.  
   - **What to do:** Culture, treat fungus if present, topical steroid + antifungal, avoid moisture.

3. **Myringitis / Perforation of TM** – rarely, severe OE can cause TM inflammation (myringitis bullosa) with bullae that rupture.  
   - **Presentation:** Sudden sharp pain followed by bloody otorrhea.  
   - **Key signs:** TM bullae (if you can see), or perforation.  
   - **What to do:** Usually self-limiting; keep ear dry, avoid drops containing ototoxic drugs (use ciprofloxacin only).

4. **Furuncle of the Canal** – localised abscess in outer cartilaginous canal (hair follicle).  
   - **Presentation:** Severe pain, can be mistaken for OE.  
   - **Key signs:** A single, localized, painful swelling in the lateral canal; no diffuse oedema.  
   - **What to do:** Incision and drainage, topical or oral anti-staphylococcal antibiotic (e.g. flucloxacillin 500mg PO qds for 5 days).

**Late-Presentation Pattern (Exam Stem):**  
A 68-year-old diabetic man with 3 weeks of right ear pain, treated by GP with neomycin drops twice, still painful. Now has mild right facial droop. What is the diagnosis and next step? → Malignant OE. Urgent CT temporal bone + ENT referral.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **TRAGUS:** Tragal tenderness, Red canal, Abscess (localised), Granulation (warning), Underlying skin disease, Swimmer’s history. → Think OE.
- **MEDS for Otitis Externa:**  
  - **M**oisture avoid  
  - **E**ar wick if occluded  
  - **D**rops (ciprofloxacin)  
  - **S**teroids for itching

**Five–Seven Clinical Pearls:**

1. The most reliable sign to differentiate OE from OM is **tragal tenderness** – not TM appearance (which you may not see).
2. If you suspect malignant OE, the earliest sign is **granulation tissue at the floor of the canal** – look for it during otoscopy.
3. Cotton buds are the enemy – they push debris deeper, macerate skin, and cause trauma. Ask patients to stop.
4. In chronic OE unresponsive to antibiotics, culture for **Aspergillus** (look for black fungal colonies). Treat with clotrimazole drops.
5. Never use antibiotic drops containing **aminoglycosides (gentamicin, neomycin, framycetin)** if there is any chance of TM perforation – risk of permanent hearing loss. Ciprofloxacin is safe.
6. **Icicles and swimming** – ear plugs + a cap reduce risk, but drying canals with a hairdryer on low is even more effective.
7. **Topical steroid alone (e.g. Locorten-Vioform)** is curative for many mild cases of OE because inflammation is the main driver; antibiotics are only needed if frank infection (purulent discharge, pus).

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**

- ❌ Prescribing oral antibiotics for OE without trying topical first.
- ❌ Forgetting to remove debris before drops – drops cannot penetrate a lump of wax.
- ❌ Assuming bilateral ear pain is OE; think systemic dermatosis (eczema, psoriasis) or fungal.
- ❌ Missing facial nerve palsy in a diabetic with ear pain – immediate referral.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to Call Senior / ENT / ICU:**

| Situation | Trigger | Action |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| **Suspected malignant OE** | Any of: diabetic/immunocompromised + severe pain >2 weeks + granulation tissue in canal + cranial nerve palsy (especially VII). | Urgent ENT referral, CT temporal bone, IV antibiotics, blood cultures, strict glucose control. ENT will likely arrange MRI and surgery. |
| **Facial nerve palsy** | New-onset ipsilateral facial weakness + ear symptoms. | Same as above – malignant OE until proven otherwise. |
| **Systemic sepsis** | Fever >38.5°C, rigors, hypotension, raised lactate, high NEWS2 (>5). | Admit, IV fluids, IV antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam), septic screen, ICU if shock. |
| **Meningism** | Headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, change in consciousness. | Meningitis may complicate malignant OE. Urgent CT head + lumbar puncture, IV antibiotics covering *Pseudomonas* (e.g. ceftazidime + metronidazole), ICU if deteriorating. |
| **Airway concern** | Massive swelling of pinna and periauricular tissue causing trismus or dysphagia. | Rare, but possible in severe cellulitis. High dependency unit, IV antibiotics, ENT for possible drainage. |

**Specific Thresholds to Monitor:**
- **NEWS2**: Score ≥5 → physician review; ≥7 → critical care outreach.
- **Lactate**: >2 mmol/L – consider sepsis; >4 – likely septic shock.
- **GCS**: ≤14 – assess for intracranial spread.
- **CRP**: Rising despite 48h of topical treatment → consider systemic infection or malignant OE.

**When in doubt, ENT is your friend – a phone call early is better than a crisis later.**
